topics.title: The real myth of Medusa — and why she was a victim, not a monster
topics.hook: Medusa was not born a monster. She was made into one by the goddess who was supposed to protect her.
topics.body: In Ovid's Metamorphoses, Medusa was once a beautiful priestess in Athena's temple — a mortal woman of extraordinary beauty who had sworn a vow of chastity in service to the goddess. Poseidon, god of the sea, violated that vow by assaulting her within the temple walls. Athena's response was not to punish the god. It was to transform the victim. Medusa's hair became serpents. Her gaze turned the living to stone. She was cast out, cursed, and made to live in exile — punished for the crime committed against her. Centuries of retellings painted her as the villain. The Greeks knew the real story.

topics.title: Loki's punishment — the truth behind the Norse trickster god
topics.hook: Loki was not always the villain of Norse mythology. He was beloved by the gods — until the day he went too far.
topics.body: Loki began as a companion of Odin — clever, quick-witted, and useful. He helped the gods build Asgard. He retrieved Thor's hammer. He solved problems the other gods could not. But when Loki arranged the death of Baldur — the most beloved of all the gods — through a trick using mistletoe, the Aesir's tolerance ended. The gods captured Loki and bound him in a cave deep within the earth, chaining him with the entrails of his own son. A serpent was placed above him, dripping venom onto his face. His wife Sigyn sat beside him with a bowl, catching the poison. When she turned away to empty it, the drops burned him — and Norse myth explains earthquakes as Loki writhing in his chains beneath the ground.

topics.title: Why Zeus was actually terrifying — the dark side of the king of gods
topics.hook: Zeus is shown in statues as noble and wise. The actual myths tell a very different story.
topics.body: Zeus overthrew his father Cronos, who had swallowed his children to prevent them from taking his throne. This is the origin story — and it sets the pattern for everything that follows. Zeus was vengeful, capricious, and violent. He chained Prometheus to a rock for eternity, having an eagle eat his regenerating liver every day, because Prometheus gave fire to humanity. He flooded the entire world, killing nearly everyone, because mortals angered him. He transformed his lovers and victims into animals, trees, and stars on a whim. He ruled by fear as much as by love. The Olympian hierarchy was not a democracy. It was a divine monarchy with all of the tyranny that implies.

topics.title: The Aztec god who demanded blood — Huitzilopochtli explained
topics.hook: The Aztec empire sacrificed thousands of people each year to keep the sun rising. This is the god they were feeding.
topics.body: Huitzilopochtli was the Aztec god of war and the sun — born fully armored from his mother Coatlicue's womb, already fighting. The Aztec cosmology held that the sun required blood to rise each day. Without sacrifice, the sun would stop. Darkness would swallow everything. This was not metaphor — the Aztecs believed this literally. Their entire civilization was organized around keeping the cosmic machinery running through human blood. The Templo Mayor in Tenochtitlan was Huitzilopochtli's home, and it was estimated that the dedication ceremony for its expansion in 1487 saw thousands sacrificed over four days. The flower wars — conflicts fought deliberately to take prisoners rather than territory — were conducted specifically to feed Huitzilopochtli.

topics.title: Anubis — Egyptian god of death and what the afterlife actually looked like
topics.hook: The Egyptian afterlife was not a heaven or a hell. It was a courtroom — and Anubis was the judge.
topics.body: Anubis, jackal-headed god of embalming and the dead, presided over the most important moment in Egyptian theology: the Weighing of the Heart. In the Hall of Two Truths, after death, your heart was placed on a scale against the Feather of Ma'at — the feather of truth and justice. Thoth recorded the outcome. Forty-two gods sat in judgment as you recited the Negative Confession — a list of 42 sins you swore you had not committed. If your heart was lighter than or equal to the feather, you passed into the Field of Reeds — an idealized version of Egypt, paradise. If your heart was heavier — weighted by sin and selfishness — it was eaten by Ammit: part lion, part hippopotamus, part crocodile. And you ceased to exist entirely. No hell. Just obliteration.

topics.title: Thor vs Odin — who was actually more powerful in Norse mythology
topics.hook: Thor was stronger. Odin was wiser. But wisdom and strength are not the same kind of power.
topics.body: Thor was the strongest being in the Norse cosmos. He wrestled the Midgard Serpent. He once came close to draining an ocean. He killed giants by the hundreds. Nobody disputed his physical supremacy. But Odin was something different — a manipulator of fate itself. He sacrificed his eye at Mimir's Well for cosmic wisdom. He hung himself from Yggdrasil for nine days to discover the runes. He could shape-shift, see the future, and move events across time. In Norse theology, Odin does not attend Ragnarok as a warrior. He attends as an orchestrator — every death, every betrayal, every hero he cultivated was a move in a game whose outcome he already knew and chose not to prevent. Odin is the scarier god. Not because of strength. Because of strategy.

topics.title: The myth of Sisyphus — and what it means philosophically
topics.hook: A man condemned to push a boulder up a hill forever, only to watch it roll back down. Albert Camus said we must imagine him happy.
topics.body: Sisyphus was a Greek king who cheated death — twice. The first time, he captured Thanatos, the god of death itself, and chained him, so no one could die. The second time, he convinced Persephone to let him return to the living to punish his wife for improper burial rites — then simply refused to go back. When Zeus finally recaptured him, the punishment was designed as the ultimate torture: push a boulder up an eternal hill that always rolls back down. Camus used Sisyphus in 1942 as the hero of absurdism. The boulder is meaningless labor — like human existence in a universe with no inherent purpose. The hill always wins. But Sisyphus chooses to walk back down and begin again. That choice — defiant, willful, human — is everything. One must imagine Sisyphus happy.

topics.title: Persephone — the real story of how winter was created
topics.hook: The ancient Greeks explained winter as a mother's grief. Here is the real myth — and it is darker than you were told.
topics.body: Persephone was the daughter of Demeter, goddess of the harvest. She was gathering flowers in a meadow when the earth split open and Hades, lord of the dead, rose and took her. No warning. No negotiation. Just absence. Demeter's grief was so total that she stopped the earth's fertility — crops died, animals stopped bearing young, and humanity began to starve. Zeus was forced to negotiate. Hades agreed to return Persephone — but before she left, she had eaten six pomegranate seeds in the underworld. Ancient law held: eat in a realm and you belong to it. Six seeds meant six months in the underworld each year. When Persephone descends, Demeter grieves and withdraws her gifts. When Persephone returns, spring comes. Every winter since is a mother's sorrow.

topics.title: Ares vs Mars — how Rome completely changed the god of war
topics.hook: The Greek Ares was despised by the other gods. The Roman Mars was one of the most honored. Same role, completely different god.
topics.body: In Greek mythology, Ares was the god of bloodlust, violence, and chaos. Even his parents — Zeus and Hera — disliked him. He was associated with slaughter for its own sake, not for any noble cause. He lost battles. He was wounded. Athena, goddess of strategic warfare, was considered his superior. The Greeks had no affection for Ares. But when Rome absorbed Greek mythology, Mars became something entirely different. Mars was the father of Romulus and Remus — the legendary founders of Rome. He was the second most powerful deity in the Roman pantheon, behind only Jupiter. He was associated with honorable warfare, military discipline, and the growth of civilization through strength. Rome was his project. The month of March is named for him.

topics.title: Hercules's 12 labors — and why he was punished in the first place
topics.hook: Hercules did not volunteer for the 12 labors. He was doing penance for murder — murder that was not his fault.
topics.body: Heracles — Hercules to the Romans — was the son of Zeus and a mortal woman, Alcmene. Hera hated him from birth because he was evidence of her husband's infidelity. She tormented him throughout his life. One night, she drove him temporarily insane. In his madness, he killed his own wife and children, believing they were enemies. When the madness lifted and he saw what he had done, Heracles was destroyed by grief. The Oracle at Delphi told him he must serve King Eurystheus for twelve years and complete whatever labors were assigned — impossible tasks designed to kill him. The Nemean Lion. The Lernaean Hydra. The Augean Stables. Cerberus himself. He completed them all. And in completing them, he earned not just forgiveness — but apotheosis. He became a god.

topics.title: Ra and the secret name — why knowing a god's true name meant power over them
topics.hook: In Egyptian mythology, your true name was your soul. And Isis stole Ra's.
topics.body: Ra was the supreme solar deity of Egypt — the god of gods. But Isis, goddess of magic and wisdom, wanted his power. She crafted a plan. Ra was elderly, and as he walked, a drop of his saliva fell to earth. Isis mixed it with clay and formed a serpent, which she hid in Ra's path. The serpent bit him. The divine poison spread through him even as the other gods tried and failed to heal it. Only Isis could cure it — but she demanded his secret name in return. Ra struggled. His secret name was his deepest identity, his true soul. Finally, in agony, he whispered it into Isis's heart, not her ear — a transfer of power, not just information. She healed him. And from that day, Isis held power over Ra himself. In Egypt, to know a name was to own it.

topics.title: The Trojan War — what Homer left out
topics.hook: The Trojan War started at a wedding that nobody wanted to invite trouble to. Trouble came anyway.
topics.body: Before the Iliad begins, there is the Judgment of Paris — the moment the war truly started. Three goddesses: Hera, Athena, and Aphrodite. One golden apple inscribed: for the fairest. Zeus refused to judge. He sent them to Paris, a Trojan prince. Each goddess offered a bribe. Hera offered political power. Athena offered military genius. Aphrodite offered the most beautiful woman in the world. Paris chose Aphrodite. The most beautiful woman was Helen — who was already married to Menelaus, King of Sparta. What followed was not purely love. Paris took Helen with divine assistance and the backing of Aphrodite. Menelaus called in every Greek king's oath of allegiance. And ten years of war began over a divine beauty contest no one was supposed to enter.

topics.title: Ragnarok — the Norse end of the world and why the gods let it happen
topics.hook: The Norse gods knew the world would end. They knew exactly how. And they prepared for it anyway.
topics.body: Ragnarok — the twilight of the gods — was not a surprise in Norse mythology. The Norns had woven its outcome into fate. Odin knew. He cultivated the Einherjar — warriors who died in battle, collected by Valkyries, brought to Valhalla to train eternally — specifically to fight in Ragnarok. He knew they would not win. Fenrir would break his chains and swallow Odin whole. Thor would kill the Midgard Serpent and die from its venom after nine steps. Loki would be released and lead the dead against Asgard. The world would be swallowed by ocean and fire. But then — new land would rise from the water. Surviving gods would rebuild. The world would begin again. Ragnarok was not purely destruction. It was a reset — a myth that acknowledged even gods could not live forever.

topics.title: Prometheus — the god who loved humans more than the other gods did
topics.hook: He was not human. But he chose human beings over the gods who created him. And he suffered for it for eternity.
topics.body: Prometheus was a Titan — one of the elder gods who preceded the Olympians. He and his brother Epimetheus were tasked with equipping all living creatures. Epimetheus gave animals their gifts first — speed to the deer, strength to the bear, feathers to the birds — until nothing was left for humans. Prometheus went to Olympus and stole fire. He brought it to humanity, teaching them warmth, craft, and civilization. Zeus's punishment was total. Prometheus was chained to a mountain, and every day an eagle devoured his liver. Every night it regrew. He endured this for thousands of years — some say until Heracles freed him. The myth endures because it is about someone who chose compassion over self-preservation. He knew the punishment before he acted. He acted anyway.

topics.title: The Valkyries — death angels or something darker
topics.hook: The Valkyries are often shown as noble warrior-maidens escorting heroes to paradise. The original Norse texts are considerably darker.
topics.body: In early Norse poetry, the Valkyries were not benevolent escorts. They were choosers of the slain — divine figures who did not simply collect the dead but decided who died. They rode above battlefields on horseback, their spears dripping with blood, their ravens circling. The Prose Edda describes them weaving with intestines as threads and severed heads as loom weights. Later romanticization softened them into warrior-maidens who brought worthy heroes to Valhalla to feast forever. But beneath the romanticized surface, the Valkyries were instruments of Odin's will — and Odin was always preparing for Ragnarok. Every hero they selected was a soldier being conscripted into a war the gods had not yet revealed.

topics.title: Set and Osiris — the original murder mystery of ancient Egypt
topics.hook: Three thousand years before Hamlet, ancient Egypt told the story of a king murdered by his brother — and the son who came to avenge him.
topics.body: Osiris was the first king of Egypt — wise, beloved, and just. His brother Set was jealous. Set constructed a beautiful coffin, announced it as a gift, and invited Osiris to lie inside to see if it fit. When Osiris lay down, Set and 72 conspirators sealed the lid and threw it into the Nile. Isis — Osiris's devoted wife — searched everywhere. She found the coffin, brought it home. Set found it again, dismembered Osiris into 14 pieces, and scattered them across Egypt. Isis retrieved 13 of them. She reconstructed the body, used her magic to briefly reanimate it, and conceived Horus — the falcon-headed heir. Horus grew up to battle Set for dominion over Egypt in a conflict lasting 80 years. In the end, Osiris was resurrected as king of the dead — and every dead Egyptian pharaoh became Osiris.

topics.title: Coyote — the trickster god of Native American mythology and what he represents
topics.hook: Across dozens of different Native American cultures, the same figure appears — a clever, foolish, creative, destructive force who breaks every rule.
topics.body: Coyote is not the god of a single tribe — he appears in mythology across hundreds of Native American nations, each with their own version. He is the archetypal trickster: clever enough to outwit the powerful, foolish enough to constantly undermine himself. In some traditions, Coyote created humans. In others, he stole fire for them — mirroring Prometheus. In others still, he causes catastrophes through his own arrogance and greed. The trickster figure is not a villain. Anthropologists note that trickster deities serve a specific cultural function — they violate the rules that define a society's boundaries, and in doing so, reveal where those boundaries are and why they exist. Coyote is chaos in service of order.

topics.title: The Olympians before Olympus — the Titans and the War in Heaven
topics.hook: Before Zeus ruled Olympus, the Titans ruled the cosmos. And the war that ended their reign makes the later Greek myths look peaceful.
topics.body: In the beginning was Chaos. From it emerged Gaia — the Earth — who bore Uranus, the Sky. Uranus fathered the Titans on Gaia but feared their power, imprisoning them inside her. Gaia, in pain, gave her youngest son Cronos a sickle and urged him to castrate his father. Cronos did — and from the blood and sea-foam that fell, Aphrodite emerged. Cronos took power and was told his own child would overthrow him, so he swallowed each child his wife Rhea bore. Rhea saved Zeus by handing Cronos a stone wrapped in cloth. Zeus grew in hiding, returned, and forced Cronos to vomit up his siblings — Hera, Poseidon, Demeter, Hestia, Hades — all fully grown and furious. The Titanomachy — the ten-year war between Titans and Olympians — followed. The Olympians won. The Titans were imprisoned in Tartarus. A new order began.

topics.title: Quetzalcoatl — the feathered serpent god who shaped all of Mesoamerica
topics.hook: He was a god who opposed human sacrifice. In a civilization built on blood, that made him dangerous — and eventually disposable.
topics.body: Quetzalcoatl — the Feathered Serpent — is one of the oldest and most widespread deities in Mesoamerican religion, appearing in Teotihuacan, Toltec, Maya, and Aztec cultures. In Aztec mythology, he was god of wind, knowledge, and the morning star, and he was credited with creating humanity in the current age by descending to the underworld and retrieving the bones of the previous age's people, mixing them with his own blood. He reportedly opposed human sacrifice, preferring offerings of flowers, incense, and butterflies. His rival Tezcatlipoca — the smoking mirror, god of conflict — tricked Quetzalcoatl into a drunken shaming that led to his exile. He sailed east on a raft of serpents, promising to return. When Cortes arrived from the east in 1519, the Aztec emperor Moctezuma may have wondered if the prophecy had come true.

topics.title: The myth of Narcissus — the psychology behind the ancient story
topics.hook: Narcissus did not fall in love with himself. He fell in love with a reflection he did not recognize — and that distinction changes everything.
topics.body: Narcissus was a beautiful youth who rejected all who loved him, including the nymph Echo — who had been cursed by Hera to only repeat others' words, never speak her own. She wasted away until only her voice remained. Nemesis, goddess of retribution, heard the prayers of those Narcissus rejected and led him to a pool. He saw a beautiful face in the water. He fell in love. He could not understand why the face would not respond to him — it mirrored his expressions but offered nothing. He could not leave. He died at the water's edge. The myth is not simply a story about vanity — it is about the inability to connect. Narcissus could not love others, and in the end could not escape himself. The pool was a prison. Echo, invisible beside him, repeated his last cry of love back to him as he died.

topics.title: Odin's sacrifice — why the Norse All-Father mutilated himself for wisdom
topics.hook: Odin was already the king of the gods. But wisdom mattered more to him than comfort, status, or even his own body.
topics.body: Odin sacrificed one eye at Mimir's Well — the well of cosmic wisdom located beneath one of the roots of Yggdrasil, the world tree — in exchange for a drink of its waters. He gave up half his sight for insight beyond mortal comprehension. He also hung himself from a branch of Yggdrasil for nine days and nine nights, wounded by his own spear, as an act of self-sacrifice to himself as a god. On the ninth day, in pain, exhausted, near death, the runes revealed themselves to him — the symbols of writing and magic that would underpin Norse cosmology. Odin pursued knowledge at costs no other god was willing to pay. That obsession shaped him: he was the god of wisdom, yes, but also of death, poetry, war, sorcery, and fate. Wisdom in Norse tradition was not gentle. It was paid for in suffering.

topics.title: The Minotaur — the monster in the maze and the sin that created it
topics.hook: The Minotaur was a monster born from a king's broken promise to a god. Athens paid the price for decades.
topics.body: Poseidon sent King Minos of Crete a perfect white bull as a sign of divine favor, with one condition: sacrifice it back to him. Minos kept the bull — it was too beautiful to kill. Poseidon's revenge was precise and devastating. He caused Minos's wife Pasiphae to develop an obsession for the bull. The craftsman Daedalus built a hollow wooden cow for her. The result of this union was the Minotaur — half man, half bull, capable of nothing but violence. Minos had Daedalus build the Labyrinth: an inescapable maze beneath the palace of Knossos. Athens was forced to send 14 young people annually as tribute — food for the monster. Theseus volunteered to go among them, entered the Labyrinth with a ball of thread given by Minos's daughter Ariadne, killed the Minotaur, and followed the thread back out.

topics.title: Freya vs Frigg — why Norse mythology has two mother goddesses
topics.hook: Norse mythology has two powerful female deities who seem to overlap. Modern scholars think they may have originally been the same goddess.
topics.body: Freya is the Vanir goddess of love, beauty, war, and death — she receives half of the battlefield slain before Odin takes his share. She travels in a chariot pulled by cats, owns a cloak of falcon feathers, and weeps gold tears for her absent husband Od. Frigg is Odin's wife, queen of Asgard, goddess of motherhood, foreknowledge, and fate — she knows everything that will happen but tells no one. The overlap is striking: both are married to Odin or an Odin-like figure, both have connections to death and the afterlife, both weep for absent husbands. The theory is that Freya and Frigg descended from a single Proto-Germanic goddess who split into two figures as the mythology evolved across cultures. They are aspects of a divine feminine too complex for a single name.

topics.title: The myth of Orpheus and Eurydice — the one rule he could not follow
topics.hook: He descended into the underworld for love. He received the one thing no mortal had ever received. And he lost it for exactly the reason you already suspect.
topics.body: Orpheus was the greatest musician who ever lived — son of the muse Calliope, taught by Apollo himself. His music could move rocks and tame wild animals. When his wife Eurydice died from a serpent bite on their wedding day, Orpheus descended into the underworld and played for Hades and Persephone. He played grief made into music so beautiful that even the Furies wept. Hades agreed to release Eurydice on one condition: Orpheus must lead her back to the surface without turning to look at her until they had both fully emerged. He led. She followed. As he stepped into sunlight, he turned — whether from doubt, from joy, from the unbearable need to confirm she was really there. Eurydice had not yet crossed the threshold. She was pulled back into death. He spent his remaining years alone, playing only songs of loss.

topics.title: The Aztec creation myth — five suns and the world built on destruction
topics.hook: The Aztecs believed our world was the fifth attempt. The previous four had all been destroyed — sometimes by flood, sometimes by wind, sometimes by fire.
topics.body: In the Aztec Legend of the Five Suns, the gods created the world four times, and each time it was destroyed. The First Sun was jaguar-ruled and eaten by jaguars. The Second Sun was blown away by wind. The Third was rained into fire. The Fourth was flooded. The Fifth Sun — ours — required the most extreme birth. The gods gathered at Teotihuacan in darkness. Two divine volunteers were needed to throw themselves into a sacred fire to become the sun and moon. The arrogant god Tecuciztecatl flinched four times. The humble, diseased god Nanahuatzin threw himself in without hesitation. Tecuciztecatl followed. Both became heavenly bodies — but the sun moved while the moon did not. The gods drew blood to set the sun in motion. This is why the Aztec universe required blood: it had been powered by sacrifice from its very first moment.

topics.title: Anansi the Spider — the trickster god who owns all stories
topics.hook: Anansi the spider god did not steal fire for humans. He stole something more valuable: all the stories in the world.
topics.body: Anansi comes from Akan mythology of West Africa and spread throughout the Caribbean and Americas through the transatlantic slave trade — one of the few pieces of cultural heritage that survived the crossing. In the foundational myth, Nyame, the sky god, owned all the stories in the world. Anansi wanted them. Nyame named his price: capture Onini the Python, Osebo the Leopard, and Mmoboro the Hornets — all seemingly impossible tasks for a spider. Anansi accomplished each through cunning, flattery, and wit rather than strength. Nyame was impressed enough to grant all stories to Anansi. From then on, all stories are called Spider Stories. Anansi myths served a deeply practical function during slavery: they celebrated cleverness over power, showed that the weak could outwit the strong, and preserved hope and identity through storytelling.

topics.title: The Flood Myth — why nearly every culture independently invented the same story
topics.hook: Noah and the ark is one version. But the same story — a righteous man, a massive flood, and a new beginning — appears in over 200 cultures independently.
topics.body: The Epic of Gilgamesh — written nearly 2,000 years before the Book of Genesis — contains a flood narrative with startling similarities: the gods decide to destroy humanity, a righteous man is warned, a boat is built, animals are loaded, the flood comes, the boat rests on a mountain, birds are released to find dry land. In Hindu mythology, the god Vishnu warns Manu of a coming flood and tells him to build a boat. In Greek myth, Deucalion and Pyrrha survive a Zeus-sent deluge. In Aztec mythology, Tata and Nene are warned by a god to hide in a hollow tree. The universality suggests either a shared origin story from a traumatic geological event in human prehistory — possibly the flooding of the Black Sea basin around 7000 BCE — or a pattern so fundamental to human experience that cultures independently arrived at it.

topics.title: Hades — why the god of the dead was actually one of the fairest Greek gods
topics.hook: Hades is often cast as the villain of Greek mythology. Read the actual myths, and he is one of the few gods who consistently behaves with dignity.
topics.body: Hades rarely appears in Greek myth, and when he does, he is almost always doing his job: judging the dead and maintaining order in the underworld. He is not cruel for sport the way Zeus is. He does not manipulate and betray the way Hera does. He does not deceive the way Apollo does. He accepted the lot he was given — the underworld, while his brothers took sky and sea — and governs it justly. The shades of the dead, good and evil alike, receive fair judgment. When heroes descend to the underworld seeking favors, Hades usually grants them if a compelling case is made. His reputation as a monster comes largely from conflating him with death itself — but Hades is not death. Death is Thanatos. Hades is just the one who rules over what comes after.

topics.title: The myth of Icarus — what the story is actually warning you about
topics.hook: Everyone knows Icarus flew too high and his wings melted. Nobody mentions that his father also warned him not to fly too low.
topics.body: Daedalus, the master craftsman who built the Labyrinth, was imprisoned on Crete with his son Icarus by King Minos — who did not want the architect of his maze to reveal its secrets. Daedalus crafted wings of feathers and wax for them both to escape. His instructions were specific: fly neither too high, where the sun's heat would melt the wax, nor too low, where sea spray would dampen the feathers and weigh them down. The middle path. Icarus, exhilarated by flight, climbed higher and higher. The wax melted. He fell. The story is almost always taught as a parable against ambition and pride. But the full warning is about the extremes — both reckless ambition and excessive caution are equally fatal. Daedalus flew the middle path alone and survived.

topics.title: Izanagi and Izanami — the Japanese creation myth and the origin of death
topics.hook: Japanese mythology explains death as a broken promise between a husband and wife. Once broken, it could never be undone.
topics.body: In Shinto creation mythology, Izanagi and Izanami were the divine couple who created the Japanese islands by stirring the primordial ocean with a jeweled spear. They descended to earth and had many divine children, including the islands of Japan and various nature deities. But when Izanami gave birth to the fire deity Kagutsuchi, she was burned to death. Izanagi, consumed with grief, descended into Yomi — the underworld — to retrieve her. She told him to wait, not to look at her while she negotiated her release. He could not wait. He lit a tooth of his comb as a torch and looked. What he saw was his wife's body rotting, crawling with maggots, transformed by death. He fled. Izanami, furious and humiliated, sent demons to pursue him. He sealed the entrance to Yomi with a great stone. She called from the other side: I will kill 1,000 people each day. He answered: Then I will create 1,500. And that, in Japanese mythology, is the origin of both death and birth.
